name: Flask
what_it_does: Lightweight Python WSGI microframework for building web applications and APIs with minimal boilerplate, relying on extensions for features like auth, database, and validation.
use_cases: Building simple REST API endpoints that agents call, with full control over request/response structure
Rapid prototyping of agent tool servers where minimal overhead and quick setup matter
Serving lightweight agent backends where async performance is not required
Creating small internal APIs for agent orchestration with custom routing logic
Building webhook receivers that trigger agent workflows on external events
not_for: High-throughput async API workloads — use FastAPI with uvicorn for async performance
Full-stack applications needing ORM, admin UI, and auth batteries-included — use Django
Large teams needing enforced structure and built-in data validation — FastAPI with Pydantic is safer
best_when: You want a minimal, no-magic Python web framework where you control everything and need to get a small API or tool server running quickly without opinionated defaults.
avoid_when: You need automatic API documentation generation, built-in async support, or type-validated request/response handling — FastAPI provides all of these out of the box.

auth.notes: Library — no auth required to install or use. Auth is entirely the developer's responsibility via extensions like Flask-Login, Flask-JWT-Extended, or Flask-Security.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free and open source — BSD licensed, no usage limits
pricing.notes: Flask is BSD-3-Clause licensed open source maintained by the Pallets project. Completely free with no commercial restrictions.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Flask returns basic HTTP error responses without structured JSON by default. Developers must implement custom error handlers with @app.errorhandler to return machine-readable JSON errors. Without customization, agents receive HTML error pages that are difficult to parse programmatically.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No built-in idempotency support — fully the developer's responsibility at the route handler level.
known_agent_gotchas: Default error responses are HTML, not JSON — agents must handle or developers must add custom JSON error handlers explicitly
No automatic request validation — agents calling Flask APIs may receive 500 errors for malformed inputs rather than 422 with details
No built-in OpenAPI spec generation — agents cannot auto-discover endpoints without flask-smorest, apiflask, or flasgger extensions
Flask development server (flask run) is not suitable for production — agents in prod must ensure gunicorn/uWSGI is used
Thread-safety of the application context (g, request) can cause subtle bugs when Flask is used in concurrent agent workflows

security_score_components.security_notes: Flask has minimal built-in security. SECRET_KEY must be set for sessions. No CSRF protection by default (add Flask-WTF). No auth enforcement. TLS handled externally by reverse proxy. Extension ecosystem varies in security maturity. Flask-Talisman adds security headers.
